---
title: "Introduction to Data Analysis with R"
subtitle: "Session 3: Data Manipulation with dplyr"
author: "Selina Baldauf"
institute: "Freie Universität Berlin - Theoretical Ecology"
date: today
date-format: long
format: 
  revealjs:
    footer: "Selina Baldauf // Session 3: Data Manipulation with dplyr"
    highlight-style: breeze
    mainfont: Cabinet Grotesk
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    incremental: true
    self-contained: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    auto-stretch: false
    fig-align: center
    fig-width: 6.3
    fig-height: 6.3
    theme: css/slides.scss
execute: 
  echo: true
knitr: 
  opts_chunk: 
    collapse: true
    comment: "#>" 
from: markdown+emoji
filters:
  - roughnotation
  - flourish
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_grey(base_size = 16))
options(dplyr.print_max = 5, pillar.print_max = 5)
```

## Recap: Session 2

:::{.nonincremental}

- **ggplot skeleton**:
```{r}
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data,
  aes(x = ..., y = ..., color, ...)
) +
  geom_xxx()

```

- **Geoms**: `geom_point()`, `geom_boxplot()`, `geom_histogram()`
- **Beautifying**: `labs()`, `scale_color_*()`, themes
- **Saving**: `ggsave()`

:::

. . .

Any questions from last time?

## Plan for today

Your data is rarely in the exact shape you need for analysis or plotting.

. . .

It requires some **data manipulation**:

:::{.nonincremental}

- Keep only certain rows or columns
- Remove missing values
- Create new variables from existing ones
- Calculate summary statistics
- ...

:::

. . .

The package in the `tidyverse` for data manipulation is called `dplyr`.

```{r}
#| eval: false
library(tidyverse)
# library(dplyr) # if you only want dplyr
```

# Data manipulation with dplyr {.inverse background-image="img/hex-stickers/dplyr.png" background-size="12vw" background-position="90% 75%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

> Transform your data step by step

## The `msleep` dataset

Remember the mammal sleep data from last session:

```{r}
msleep
```


## `filter()`: pick rows

Keep only rows that match a condition:

. . .

```{r}
filter(msleep, vore == "carni")
```

. . .

:::{.callout-note}

## To reuse, save result in a variable

```{r}
carnivores <- filter(msleep, vore == "carni")
```

:::

## `filter()`: comparison operators

:::{.nonincremental}

- `==` equal to
- `!=` not equal to
- `<`, `>` less than, greater than
- `<=`, `>=` less than or equal to, greater than or equal to
- `%in%` in a set of values

:::

. . .

Other examples

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Filter for sleep time greater than 16 hours
filter(msleep, sleep_total > 16)
# All rows except carnivores
filter(msleep, vore != "carni")
# All rows where vore is herbivore, carnivore or omnivore
filter(msleep, vore %in% c("herbi", "carni", "omni"))
```

## `filter()`: combine conditions

Use `&` (and) - both conditions must be true:

. . .

```{r}
filter(msleep, vore == "herbi" & sleep_total > 12)
```

## `filter()`: combine conditions

Use `|` (or) - one conditions must be true:

```{r}
# Either condition must be true
filter(msleep, vore == "herbi" | vore == "omni")
```

## `filter_out()`: opposite of filter

Relatively new function - keeps rows that **don't** match the condition:

```{r}
# Keep rows that are NOT herbivores
filter_out(msleep, vore == "herbi")
```

. . .

<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
# equivalent to
filter(msleep, vore != "herbi")
```

## `select()`: pick columns

Keep only the columns you need:

. . .

```{r}
select(msleep, name, vore, sleep_total)
```

## `select()`: remove columns

Use `-` to remove columns:

. . .

```{r}
select(msleep, -name, -conservation)
```

## `select()`: patterns

Select helpers for patterns in column names:

```{r}
# All columns that start with "sleep"
select(msleep, starts_with("sleep"))
```

. . .

There are also `ends_with()`, `contains()`, and more - see `?select` for details.


## `mutate()`: create new columns

Add new columns based on existing ones:

. . .

```{r}
# Create a dataset with fewer columns
msleep_small <- select(msleep, name, sleep_total, awake)
mutate(msleep_small, sleep_awake_ratio = sleep_total / awake)
```


. . .

The new columns are added at the end of the tibble. The original columns are kept.

## `mutate()`: multiple columns at once

```{r}
mutate(
  msleep_small,
  sleep_awake_ratio = sleep_total / awake,
  sleep_minutes = sleep_total * 60
)
```

## `drop_na()`: remove missing values

Remove rows with `NA` values in any column:

. . .

```{r}
# Drop all rows with NAs in any column
drop_na(msleep)
```

. . .

The gapminder data has no missing values, so the filter doesn't change anything here.

## `drop_na()`: remove missing values

Remove rows with `NA` values in (a) specific column(s):

. . .

```{r}
# Drop rows with NAs in specific columns
drop_na(msleep, vore, sleep_rem)
```

. . .

`drop_na()` is part of the `tidyr` package which  is loaded with `library(tidyverse)`.

## The problem: multiple steps

Data cleaning and manipulation usually requires **several steps in sequence**.

. . .

```{r}
# Step 1: remove missing values
msleep_clean <- drop_na(msleep, vore, sleep_rem)
# Step 2: keep only herbivores
msleep_clean <- filter(msleep_clean, vore == "herbi")
# Step 3: add a new variable
msleep_clean <- mutate(msleep_clean, rem_proportion = sleep_rem / sleep_total)
```

This works, but creates lots of intermediate steps.

## The pipe `|>`

The pipe operator passes the result of one step as the **first argument** to the next step.


```{r}
#| eval: false
# This
filter(msleep, vore == "herbi")

# is the same as
msleep |> filter(vore == "herbi")
```

. . .

This is perfect for dplyr because:

:::{.nonincremental}

- First argument is the data (a tibble)
- Output (return value) is also a tibble 

:::

## The pipe `|>`

The pipe operator passes the result of one step as the **first argument** to the next step.

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

Instead of this: 

```{r}
#| eval: false
# Step 1: remove missing values
msleep_clean <- drop_na(msleep, vore, sleep_rem)
# Step 2: keep only herbivores
msleep_clean <- filter(msleep_clean, vore == "herbi")
# Step 3: add a new variable
msleep_clean <- mutate(msleep_clean, rem_proportion = sleep_rem / sleep_total)
```

:::
::: {.column}

:::{.fragment}

We can do this: 

```{r}
msleep_clean <- msleep |>
  drop_na(vore, sleep_rem) |>
  filter(vore == "herbi") |>
  mutate(rem_proportion = sleep_rem / sleep_total)
```

:::
:::
:::

. . .

<br>

Read `|>` as **"and then"**:

Take msleep, **and then** drop NAs, **and then** filter, **and then** add a column.


## The pipe `|>`

- 2 different pipes in R (work similarly):
  - `|>`: base R pipe, available in R 4.1 and later
  - `%>%`: magrittr pipe, used in older versions and still common in tidyverse code

- I prefer `|>` because it's built into R

- Turn on the base R pipe in RStudio: Tools > Global Options > Code > Editing > Use native pipe operator (`|>`)

. . .

:::{.callout-tip}
Use the keyboard shortcut `Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + M` to insert `|>`
:::


# Exercise 1 {.inverse}

15 min, [link to the exercise](../tasks/task-06-dplyr-basics.qmd)<br>

[Filter, select, and mutate the penguins data]{.highlight-blue}

:::{.nonincremental}
- Stay here to do the task
- I will be in the breakout room "Questions"
- If you have questions or problems: Join from Breakout rooms  ![](img/breakout_rooms_blue_dark.png){width=3%}  in meeting controls
:::


# Summarizing Data with dplyr {.inverse background-image="img/hex-stickers/dplyr.png" background-size="12vw" background-position="90% 75%" background-repeat="no-repeat"}

> From many rows to key numbers

## `summarize()`: collapse rows

Calculate summary statistics across all rows:

. . .

::: {.columns}
::: {.column}

Use `na.rm = TRUE` to ignore NAs:
```{r}
msleep |>
  summarize(
    mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_rem = mean(sleep_rem, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
```


::: {.fragment}

The result is a tibble with **one row** — the data is collapsed to a single summary.

:::

:::
::: {.column}

:::{.fragment}

Or remove the NAs first:

```{r}
msleep |>
  drop_na(sleep_total, sleep_rem) |>
  summarize(
    mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total),
    mean_rem = mean(sleep_rem)
  )
```

:::
:::
:::

## `summarize()` with `.by`: grouped summaries

Calculate summaries **separately for each group**:

. . .

```{r}
msleep |>
  summarize(
    mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_rem = mean(sleep_rem, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = vore
  )
```

## `summarize()` with `.by`: multiple groups

Group by more than one variable:

. . .

```{r}
msleep |>
  summarize(
    mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = c(vore, conservation)
  )
```

## `count()`: count rows per group

A shortcut for counting observations:

. . .

```{r}
msleep |>
  count(vore)
```

. . .

<br>

```{r}
msleep |>
  count(vore, conservation)
```

## Pipe into ggplot

The pipe works with ggplot too — manipulate your data and plot it in one go:

. . .

```{r}
#| fig-height: 4
msleep |>
  drop_na(vore) |>
  summarize(mean_sleep = mean(sleep_total), .by = vore) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = vore, y = mean_sleep, fill = vore)) +
  geom_col()
```

## Pipe into ggplot

You can also filter before plotting:

. . .

:::{.columns}

:::{.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| eval: false
msleep |>
  drop_na(vore, sleep_rem) |>
  filter(bodywt < 50) |>
  ggplot(
    aes(
      x = sleep_total,
      y = sleep_rem,
      color = vore
    )
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()
```

- Use `|>` between dplyr steps
- Switch to `+` once you start the ggplot

:::

:::{.column width="50%"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
msleep |>
  drop_na(vore, sleep_rem) |>
  filter(bodywt < 50) |>
  ggplot(
    aes(
      x = sleep_total,
      y = sleep_rem,
      color = vore
    )
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16)
```

:::

:::

# Exercise 2 {.inverse}

15 min, [link to the exercise](../tasks/task-07-dplyr-summarize.qmd)<br>

[Summarize data and combine dplyr with ggplot]{.highlight-blue}

:::{.nonincremental}
- Stay here to do the task
- I will be in the breakout room "Questions"
- If you have questions or problems: Join from Breakout rooms  ![](img/breakout_rooms_blue_dark.png){width=3%}  in meeting controls
:::


# Wrap up {.inverse}

## What we covered today

:::{.nonincremental}

- **`filter()`**: pick rows by condition
- **`select()`**: pick columns by name
- **`mutate()`**: create new columns
- **`drop_na()`**: remove rows with missing values
- **`summarize()`** + `.by`: grouped summaries
- **`count()`**: count observations per group
- **The pipe** `|>`: chain operations together, read top to bottom


:::

## Good to know

`dplyr` has more useful functions that we didn't cover today:

:::{.nonincremental}

- **`bind_rows()`**: combine multiple tables with the same columns into one
- **`left_join()`**: combine two tables by a shared column
- **`arrange()`**: sort rows by column values
- **rename()**: rename columns
- ...

:::

. . .

You can find documentation, examples and a cheatsheet for all of these on the [dplyr](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/) websites.

## Next time

Putting it all together — a full data analysis project from start to finish.

:::{.nonincremental}

- Read data
- Clean and transform
- Summarize
- Visualize

:::

# Thanks for your attention! {.inverse}

See you in the next session!
